summary: DeVault argues that Electron is a terrible choice for desktop application development, shipping an entire Chromium browser with every app for massive resource overhead. He catalogs several egregious examples of simple utilities built on Electron that could be trivially implemented with native tools. The post contends that developers choose Electron out of laziness rather than making informed engineering decisions about the right tool for the job. He urges JavaScript-only developers to learn native GUI toolkits and other programming languages. He concedes only two apps where Electron might be a reasonable choice. The piece is an unabashed rant from a Unix hacker's perspective about the degradation of desktop software quality.
key_points: Electron ships an entire copy of Chromium with every app, creating massive overhead in disk space, RAM, and CPU usage for often trivial functionality
Many Electron apps fail to deliver even the promised cross-platform benefit, shipping for only one OS despite the framework's supposed portability
Developers choose Electron because they only know JavaScript and are too lazy to learn proper native GUI development tools
Frontend web development is something we tolerate out of necessity, not because it's good — extending it to the desktop compounds the problem
Engineers have a professional responsibility to choose the right tool for the job rather than defaulting to what's easiest
money_quotes: We tolerate frontend web development because we have no choice, not because it is by any means good.
The reason people choose Electron is because they are too lazy to learn the right tools for the job. This is the worst quality a developer can have.
You're an engineer, for the love of God! Fucking act like one!
Do they build square airplanes so they don't have to learn about aerodynamics, then just throw on an extra ten engines to make up for it? NO!
They've taken one of the most lightweight and essential tools on your computer and bloated it by orders of magnitude.
In order to show you a list of things you've copied, it uses an entire bundled copy of Chromium.
If you're the guy who's going to leave a comment about how this blog post introduced you to a bunch of interesting apps you're going to install now, I hate you.
Learn another programming language. Fun fact: it'll make your JavaScript better.
key_insight: Choosing Electron for simple desktop applications is an act of engineering laziness that imposes massive resource costs on users for the developer's convenience of only needing to know JavaScript.
